title: QPI Basic Contract Skeleton (Official HM25 Template)
QPI Invocation Reward Handling Patterns
QPI Container Types
QPI System Procedures (Lifecycle Hooks)
QPI _WITH_LOCALS Pattern for Local Variables
QPI PUBLIC vs PRIVATE Functions/Procedures
QPI C++ Restrictions (Official from contracts.md)
content:
FORBIDDEN in QPI contracts:
- Local variables on function call stack (use _WITH_LOCALS macros)
- Loop index variables like for(int i=0;...) (use _locals struct)
- Pointers: defining, casting, dereferencing (* only allowed for multiplication)
- Characters [ and ] (no low-level arrays, use Array<T,L>)
- All preprocessor directives # (no #include, #pragma, #ifdef)
- float and double (non-deterministic arithmetic)
- Division / and modulo % operators (use div() and mod(), return 0 on div-by-zero)
- String literals " and char literals ' (can jump to random memory)
- Variadic arguments ... (parameter packs forbidden)
- Double underscores __ (reserved for internal functions)
- QpiContext and const_cast keywords
- Scope resolution :: except for contract-defined structs/enums and qpi.h
- union keyword
- typedef and using at global scope (except using namespace QPI)
- Global variables (only global constants prefixed with contract name)
- Recursion depth limit: 10 levels
- Input/output structs may only use: integer types, bit, id, Array, BitArray, and structs of these
- Collection, HashMap, HashSet FORBIDDEN in public interface (input/output structs)

title: QPI Contract Deployment Process (Official)
content:
Steps to deploy a Qubic smart contract:
1. Write contract as single .h file in src/contracts/ directory
2. Fork qubic/core repo, create branch feature/YYYY-MM-DD-YourContractName
3. Add contract in src/contract_core/contract_def.h with next unused index
4. Implement GoogleTest tests in test/contract_[name].cpp
5. Pass Qubic Contract Verification Tool (checks C++ restrictions)
6. Open PR to develop branch, request Core dev review
7. Core devs review for restricted C++ compliance and style
8. Test in multi-node testnet
9. Submit proposal to GQMPROP smart contract with description
10. Computors vote in epoch N (need 451+ votes, majority yes)
11. If accepted: IPO held in epoch N+1 (Dutch auction, QU burned for fee reserve)
12. If IPO successful: Contract constructed in epoch N+2 (state initialized, procedures callable)
Contract naming: file=YourContract.h, shares=YCN (max 7 chars), state struct=YOURCONTRACTNAME
Smart contract code is generally IMMUTABLE after deployment.

title: QPI Asset Management
Qubic RPC API Endpoints
content:
Mainnet RPC: https://rpc.qubic.org
Public Testnet RPC: https://testnet-rpc.qubicdev.com

Key endpoints:
GET /v1/status - Network stats (supply, addresses, price, marketCap)
GET /v1/tick-info - Current tick info
GET /v1/balances/{addressId} - Balance query
POST /v1/querySmartContract - Read contract state (functions)
POST /v1/broadcast-transaction - Execute contract procedures
GET /v1/transaction/{txId} - Transaction details
GET /v1/transaction-status/{txId} - Transaction status
GET /v1/tick-transactions/{tick} - Transactions in a tick
POST https://api.qubic.org/v1/events/decodeEvent - Decode contract events

For querySmartContract, input/output is Base64 encoded.
Response: { responseData: '<base64>' } - decode and parse per output struct.

title: QPI Frontend Smart Contract Interaction (JavaScript)
Qubic Oracle Machines -- Real-World Data for Smart Contracts
content:
Oracle Machines bridge smart contracts to real-world data via QPI.

Architecture (3 layers):
1. Qubic Core Nodes -- where contracts execute
2. Oracle Machine Node -- middleware (routing, caching, validation)
3. External Oracle Services -- price feeds, weather APIs, event providers

Data Flow:
Contract sends OracleMachineQuery -> Oracle Machine fetches data -> OracleMachineReply returned -> Quorum verifies via OracleReplyCommitTransaction per Computor -> OracleReplyRevealTransaction reveals on-chain.

Interface Types:
- Price Interface (Index 0): Query {Oracle, Timestamp, Currency1, Currency2} -> Reply {Numerator: sint64, Denominator: sint64}
- Mock Interface (Index 1): For testing

Two Request Modes:
- One-Time Query: single request/response
- Subscription: continuous updates at intervals

Request Tracking: unique ID per request. Status: Pending, Successful, or Failed (timeout).
Fees: burned (deflationary). Level set by Quorum.
Latency: 3-5 ticks (~10 seconds).
Caching: TTL-based with automatic cleanup.

Building Custom Oracles:
1. Define interface structures in Qubic Core (query/reply formats)
2. Inherit from BaseOracleService
3. Implement data providers for external APIs
4. Add configuration entries
5. Register in build system
Repo: github.com/qubic/oracle-machine

Use Cases: DeFi price feeds, prediction markets, parametric insurance, gaming/NFTs with real-world data.

title: Oracle-Consuming Smart Contract Pattern
POST_INCOMING_TRANSFER Callback -- Handling Incoming QU
content:
POST_INCOMING_TRANSFER (or POST_INCOMING_TRANSFER_WITH_LOCALS) notifies the contract about incoming QU transfers.

Input struct (provided by system):
struct PostIncomingTransfer_input {
    id sourceId;       // entity that sent QU
    sint64 amount;     // number of QU transferred
    uint8 type;        // transfer type (see below)
};

Transfer Types:
- TransferType::procedureTransaction -- runs after transferring invocation reward, before procedure execution
- TransferType::standardTransaction -- transaction with inputType == 0
- TransferType::qpiTransfer -- triggered by qpi.transfer()
- TransferType::qpiDistributeDividends -- triggered by qpi.distributeDividends()
- TransferType::revenueDonation -- contract receives computor revenue donation (end of epoch)
- TransferType::ipoBidRefund -- IPO bid refund via qpi.bidInIPO()

CRITICAL RULES:
- qpi.invocator() returns 0 inside POST_INCOMING_TRANSFER -- use input.sourceId
- qpi.invocationReward() returns 0 -- use input.amount
- Cannot call qpi.distributeDividends() or qpi.bidInIPO() inside callback
- qpi.transfer() only allowed to non-contract entities
- Invoking user procedures from callback executes with invocationReward() == 0

title: Vulnerability: uint64 Integer Overflow
Vulnerability: Invocation Reward Locked in Contract
Vulnerability: State Modification in PUBLIC_FUNCTION (Silent Bug)
Vulnerability: Epoch Boundary Race Conditions
HashMap/HashSet Cleanup Pattern at END_EPOCH
QPI Proposals and Voting On-Chain
content:
Proposals and voting provide on-chain decision-making implemented in contracts. Shareholders (those who own contract shares from IPO) can vote.

Key concepts:
- Proposals are submitted by shareholders
- Voting is weighted by share ownership
- Results are determined by Quorum
- Detailed API described in contracts_proposals.md

CFB has an alternative idea for proposal-free voting on shareholder variables planned for QX, RANDOM, and MLM contracts (not yet implemented).

For implementing governance:
1. Track shares via state (shareholders are tracked by system)
2. Define proposal structs for each decision type
3. Use QPI voting macros to tally votes
4. Execute approved proposals in system procedures

Ref: github.com/qubic/core/blob/main/doc/contracts_proposals.md

Mainnet Readiness Checklist for QPI Contracts
content:
Pre-mainnet checklist:
- Invocation reward paths tested (refund/accept/burn).
- PUBLIC_FUNCTION has no state mutation or transfer calls.
- REGISTER_USER_FUNCTIONS_AND_PROCEDURES is complete and index-safe.
- Overflow/underflow guards on balance/accounting math.
- END_EPOCH cleanup for HashMap/HashSet/Collection fragmentation.
- Cross-contract call direction validated by index constraints.
- Oracle/time assumptions reviewed against tick latency.
- QU reserve burn model reviewed under peak load.
- Adversarial scenarios tested (reentrancy-like callbacks, malformed input payloads).
- Proposal docs + test evidence prepared for computor review.

title: Event Logging Guidance (Procedure-Only)
Tick-Time Patterns and Time-Based Logic
Oracle Subscription Pattern (Periodic Updates)
content:
For recurring oracle-fed contracts, model subscriptions explicitly:

State:
- target pair / feed id
- refreshIntervalTicks
- nextRefreshTick
- latest value + source tick

Flow:
1) At/after nextRefreshTick, request or accept oracle update.
2) Validate denominator != 0 and staleness bounds.
3) Persist value and set nextRefreshTick = qpi.tick() + interval.

Security checks:
- Reject stale data beyond max age.
- Handle missing responses gracefully.
- Keep fallback behavior explicit (pause, hold last value, or safe default).

title: QU Burn Budgeting and Reserve Lifetime Estimation
content:
Execution consumes QU from contract reserve. Estimate runway before launch.

Simple estimator:
reserveQU = current reserve
avgBurnPerCall = measured burn
callsPerTick = expected load

burnPerTick = avgBurnPerCall * callsPerTick
runwayTicks = burnPerTick > 0 ? div(reserveQU, burnPerTick) : 0

Operational guidance:
- Keep emergency guardrails in expensive procedures.
- Expose reserve + burn metrics via PUBLIC_FUNCTION for monitoring.
- Revisit fee policy if runway under realistic peak load is too short.

title: Smart Contract Lifecycle: From Code to Mainnet
content: Full lifecycle: 1) Research & Planning (study QX, Quottery, QEARN, QVault), 2) Learn QPI guidelines and restrictions, 3) Code contract (.h file in src/contracts/), 4) Unit testing with GoogleTest, 5) Local testnet (Core Lite recommended), 6) SC Verification Tool (qubic-contract-verify), 7) Security audit (recommended), 8) Create PR to qubic/core develop branch, 9) Core dev review, 10) Computor proposal via GQMPROP (epoch N), 11) Voting phase (451 of 676 computors must participate, majority approval), 12) IPO Dutch Auction (epoch N+1, all 676 shares must sell or contract is permanently broken), 13) Contract goes live (epoch N+2). Timeline: Development weeks-months, Verification 1-2 weeks, PR review 1-3 weeks, Proposal+Voting ~7 days, IPO ~7 days. Post-launch: bug fixes via regular PR, new features require new proposal. Contract must burn QU to stay active.

title: Adding and Registering a Contract in contract_def.h
content:
To register a contract in contract_def.h:

1. Define CONTRACT_INDEX and STATE (before '// new contracts should be added above this line'):
#undef CONTRACT_INDEX
#undef CONTRACT_STATE_TYPE
#undef CONTRACT_STATE2_TYPE
#define MYTEST_CONTRACT_INDEX 13
#define CONTRACT_INDEX MYTEST_CONTRACT_INDEX
#define CONTRACT_STATE_TYPE MYTEST
#define CONTRACT_STATE2_TYPE MYTEST2
#include "contracts/MyTest.h"

2. Add contract description:
{"MYTEST", 999, 10000, sizeof(MYTEST)},
// Format: {"ASSET_NAME", CONSTRUCTION_EPOCH, DESTRUCTION_EPOCH, SIZE_OF_STATE}
// Asset name: max 7 uppercase letters/digits

3. Register in initializeContracts():
REGISTER_CONTRACT_FUNCTIONS_AND_PROCEDURES(MYTEST);

Naming: Long name for filename (MyTest.h), short name max 7 chars for asset (MYTEST), state struct full uppercase (MYTEST).

title: GoogleTest Testing Patterns for QPI Contracts
Qubic CLI: Calling Functions and Invoking Procedures
content:
Qubic CLI (github.com/qubic/qubic-cli) for interacting with core node.

Call contract function (immediate, read-only):
qubic-cli -nodeip <IP> -nodeport <PORT> -sendrawpacket <HEX> <SIZE>
Response starts from byte 8. Decode hex to output struct.

Invoke contract procedure (creates transaction, scheduled at tick):
qubic-cli -seed <SEED> -nodeip <IP> -nodeport <PORT> -sendcustomtransaction <CONTRACT_ID> <PROCEDURE_ID> <AMOUNT> <INPUT_SIZE> <INPUT_HEX>

Key difference:
- Function calls: TCP connection, immediate response
- Procedure calls: transaction sent, executed when network reaches the tick

Get contract identity:
id contract(CONTRACT_INDEX, 0, 0, 0);
CHAR16* contractAddress = new CHAR16[61];
getIdentity(contract.m256i_u8, contractAddress, false);

Cannot reuse same dejavu (input hex) twice. Regenerate for each call.
Must use seed with balance for procedure invocation.

title: Cross-Contract Calls: CALL_OTHER_CONTRACT and INVOKE_OTHER_CONTRACT
Asset Issuance, Share Transfer, and Management Rights
content:
Asset issuance:
qpi.issueAsset(assetName, issuer, decimalPlaces, numberOfShares, unitOfMeasurement)
- assetName: up to 7 uppercase A-Z chars encoded as uint64
- issuer must match caller
- Returns number of shares issued or negative on error

Share transfer (atomic ownership + possession):
qpi.transferShareOwnershipAndPossession(assetName, issuer, owner, possessor, numberOfShares, newOwnerAndPossessor)
- Use NULL_ID as newOwnerAndPossessor to burn shares

Management rights transfer:
qpi.releaseShares() - contract A sends rights to contract B
qpi.acquireShares() - contract B requests rights from contract A

PRE_ACQUIRE_SHARES / PRE_RELEASE_SHARES: validate fee and allowTransfer
POST_ACQUIRE_SHARES / POST_RELEASE_SHARES: post-transfer hooks

676 contract shares created after successful IPO.
Execution fee reserve: finalPrice * 676 from IPO proceeds.
Replenish via qpi.burn() or QUtil's BurnQubicForContract.

title: Qubic Name Service (QNS) Contract Reference
content:
QNS: decentralized naming system mapping human-readable names to Qubic addresses.

Data structures:
- UEFIString<LENGTH>: fixed-length validated string (max 32 chars, alphanumeric)
- Domain: subDomain + rootDomain + tld (.qubic or .qns)
- RegistryRecord: owner, registerDate, registerEpoch, registrationYears
- ResolveData: address (id) + text (UEFIString)

Storage: HashMap for registry and nested HashMap for resolve data.

Procedures:
- RegisterDomain: 2M QU/year, validates name and TLD
- RenewDomain: extends registration
- TransferDomain: 100 QU fee, changes owner
- SetResolveAddressData: maps domain to Qubic address (owner only)

Functions:
- GetResolveAddressData: returns mapped address

System hooks:
- INITIALIZE: sets up .qubic and .qns TLDs
- BEGIN_EPOCH: processes domain expirations, cleans stale data

Note: This is a reference implementation, not the production QNS.
